evidence: The docstring poses the problem for a set X of cardinality aleph_omega, formalized as #X = ℵ_ ω.
The docstring defines H(n) via a function f from the power set P(X) to X whose images over subsets of Y cover X.
The lemma characterizes when a triangle can be decomposed into n congruent triangles (IsCuttable uses pointwise congruence ≅).
The solved lemma states such a triangle is cuttable only into a non-zero square number of congruent triangles (IsSquare n).
The open question asks which triangles can only be decomposed into a square number of congruent triangles.
The open question's conclusion is that every admissible decomposition count n satisfies IsSquare n.
The solved lemma (from Soifer [So09]) states every triangle can be decomposed into n similar triangles except n = 0, 2, 3, 5.
The solved lemma exhibits a triangle not decomposable into 0, 2, 3 or 5 similar triangles, per Soifer [So09].
The docstring asks whether every finite graph with minimum degree at least 3 contains a cycle of length 2^k, which is precisely the Erdős–Gyárfás conjecture.
The statement requires the existence of a graph cycle (Walk.IsCycle) whose length is a power of two.
The docstring asserts a monochromatic three-term arithmetic progression x, x+d, x+2d with d > x in any 2-colouring of N.
The problem is a monochromatic-structure result about 2-colourings of N (erdosproblems.com tags it ramsey theory).
The docstring lets tau(n) count the number of divisors of n (formalized as σ 0) and asks about max_{m<n}(m + tau(m)).
The variant verifies the divisor-count bound max_{m<24}(m + tau(m)) <= 26 for n = 24.
Erdős's suggested strengthening concerns lim max_{m<n}(tau(m) + m - n) with tau the number-of-divisors function.
The variant asks for infinitely many n with max_{n-k<m<n}(m + tau(m)) <= n + 2, again about the divisor-counting function tau.
The problem asks whether the counts R(x_i) of distinct distances from each point take at least (1-o(1))n values, a variant in the distinct-distances family (erdosproblems tags: geometry, distances).
The statement asks for at least (1+c)n/2 distinct distances among planar point sets, a distinct-distances-type question; disproved by the regular n-gon.
The general-position variant again asks for (1+c)n/2 distinct distances (distinctDistances X) among valid planar point sets.
The docstring asks for n planar points where every 4-subset determines at least 3 distances yet the total distinct distances is O(n/sqrt(log n)) — a distinct-distances construction problem.
